summary: Gruber argues that macOS is losing its balance between security protections and power-user functionality, with permission prompts becoming increasingly infuriating. He traces the Mac's history from the laissez-faire classic Mac OS era to today's increasingly restrictive approach, acknowledging that many protections are necessary but warning that Apple has crossed a line. Using the analogy of SawStop table saws, he argues safety features should protect users without impeding legitimate work. He contends that users who need the level of protection Apple is now imposing on Mac users should be using iPads instead, and that the iPad's own weaknesses are partly to blame for pushing unsophisticated users onto Macs.
key_points: macOS permission prompts have shifted from reasonable safety measures to infuriating obstacles that power users must constantly dismiss, resembling what Apple once mocked in its own ads
SawStop table saws demonstrate the ideal balance: safety technology that prevents injuries without making the tool worse at its primary job, and includes a bypass mode for edge cases
The Mac is fundamentally a Unix workstation accessible to the mass market — not a locked-down appliance — and users who need appliance-level protection should be using iPads
The iPad's weaknesses are partly responsible for the Mac's over-protection problem, because users who would be better served by iPads are forced onto Macs instead
Safety features and power tools aren't mutually exclusive, but Apple is clearly losing the balance between the two on macOS
money_quotes: The Mac is a platform where you need to be able to shoot yourself in the foot.
It's infuriating to play whack-a-mole to dismiss a barrage of permission prompts to confirm the same permissions you've previously granted to the same software.
At a certain point, a hammer needs to hammer whatever it strikes, and sometimes, alas, that's the user's thumb. That's the Mac.
It is not an appliance intended to prevent any possible malware or scamware from running. Apple makes such appliances. They run iOS.
The world needs locked-down can't-cut-your-fingers-off-no-matter-what-you-do platforms like the iPad.
Any Mac user who isn't sufficiently served by the anti-malware/scamware protections already in MacOS shouldn't be using a Mac at all.
It's a Unix workstation that's friendly enough to be used by the mass market.
key_insight: Apple's increasingly aggressive macOS security prompts are degrading the Mac's core identity as a power tool, partly because the iPad's limitations force unsophisticated users onto a platform that was never meant to be an appliance.
